claim: The Zipf exponent of 0.8946 (below the natural-language baseline of ~1.0) is caused by systematic word-level homophones: multiple surface word forms encode the same plaintext word, inflating low-frequency tail counts. Collapsing homophones defined by shared word-initial bigram and word length would restore the Zipf exponent to >= 0.95.
evidence_for: Zipf exponent 0.8946 with R-squared 0.9084 indicates a moderate but suboptimal fit, consistent with artificial inflation of the low-frequency tail
Homophonic ciphers are known to flatten Zipf slopes by distributing high-frequency plaintext words across multiple surface forms
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is high relative to glyph entropy 3.8627, suggesting surface word diversity exceeds what grammar alone would produce
evidence_against: R-squared 0.9084 is still a reasonable Zipf fit; the deviation from 1.0 could reflect topic-restricted medieval vocabulary rather than cipher homophones
Collapsing by initial bigram and length is an arbitrary grouping that may not correspond to true plaintext equivalence classes
Hapax ratio 70.1% under a homophonic model should decrease toward the tail, not inflate it, unless the homophone set is very large
test: Group all unique word forms by (word-initial bigram, word length) as a proxy for plaintext equivalence classes; compute the Zipf exponent of the collapsed frequency distribution (treating each group as one token type, summing its tokens). Compare collapsed Zipf exponent and R-squared to the original.
test_metric: Zipf exponent and R-squared of the collapsed word-frequency distribution versus the original (0.8946, RÂ²=0.9084)
test_threshold: Confirm homophone hypothesis if collapsed Zipf exponent >= 0.95 and R-squared improves to >= 0.94; deny if collapsed exponent changes by < 0.02 or R-squared decreases, indicating the grouping variable does not capture real equivalence classes

reasoning: If homophones are the mechanism suppressing the Zipf exponent, then grouping by a simple cipher-structure proxy (initial bigram + length) should partially undo the suppression and recover a more natural frequency distribution. A measurable improvement in Zipf fit would be strong evidence for word-level homophony.
